speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well, anyhow that's just an incident as far as the book is concerned. So I-- you know, I got my beating and I realized that I shouldn't do it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who beat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt. I don't remember. It wasn't-- it wasn't very-- wasn't a bad beating, but I was really, from then on, I was really watched over, you know. And I realized that I shouldn't-- you know, I-- I have to be-- if I'm going to do it, I have to be more secretive. Well, anyhow, we had-- life in the ghetto, as I remember, as I mentioned to you, was really a challenge for me. And I approached it in a way, I guess, maybe it was a child's way. I remember the-- the-- the children, especially the old people when they evacuated when they partitioned off the old people. There were train cars. I remember them. And they kept-- they-- cattle trains, really. And they dragged these people into them and the screamings and the yellings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you see the railroad from your house or wherever you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I could see it, and I guess it was the-- the railroad did not go very far from-- I mean, the-- the-- the tracks were not far or was actually in the ghetto as well. And they'd bring those cars in and they were cattle-- I remember that they were little had little windows, and they would just push these people in and then there was screaming and-- and crying. And-- and I remember the faces on the Lithuanians and the Germans as even-- cold, real straight faces with as a matter of fact and even saying, OK, hurry up. And-- and even beating them or shooting them. I remember some-- if people rebelled, they would-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see someone shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. As a matter of fact, one incident which I will never forget was that they were-- the laws were circulated that no people who go out in the workforce are permitted to bring anything back. Well, they-- they said there will be punishment by death. So they caught one person, and they made a-- made him as a-- how should I say it, as a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] picture, you know, for everybody to see. So they-- they took him, and they took him out into a field and drove everybody out to-- into that big field and-- to actually witness the hanging. And I remember watching it. I remember putting the rope around-- the rope being placed on his-- on his neck. And-- and as they elevated, as they-- as he was the elevated and finally the expression on his-- and they made us look at it. But as I said, I was-- being a child, I guess I approached everything with-- with kind of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] child's point of view of-- of not breaking down, of looking at it in-- in a curiosity way. And he was left there for three days, hanging and with big signs and big--

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember that there were big horns that they would go around with, big loud speakers looks like horn and broadcasting over the ghetto. You see what happens to him? That's what will happen to you if you disobey the law. What had he brought in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought in bread and sausage. And they were hidden under his clothing. That's one way of-- of bringing the cloth-- of bringing the food in. I remember that when my cousin used to go to work, everyone had big-- like, big pockets, like a basket-- well, it wasn't a basket. It was a clothes-- like a laundry, clothes bag under your clothes. And your clothes was really designed specially for that to hide that-- that bag. And in the bag, they would place, you know, food, bread, and butter-- you know, staples to eat. So that-- various scenes like this I really remember very distinctly. Now my uncle, as I mentioned, we-- we really-- we were really almost, I would say, privileged. My uncle would go to the-- to the committee. There was a Jewish committee that was appointed right away, and the Germans and the Lithuanians would work with the Jewish committee. Well, my uncle was part of the committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was really like a dog-eat-dog life. I mean everyone was out for himself, for his family and for himself, to-- for survival. When it comes right down to it, I really felt that, you know, that you do what you have to do for survival. And even if you-- it means putting the other person in danger, as long as you survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw that in the ghetto and even to the point where-- where I suppose the Germans were forcing some of the committee members to-- to, as we call them, [? masser, ?] which means to tell on another person, as to who was-- who was bringing in lots of food. Because if they-- if the committee people or the police-- we had our own Jewish police as well-- so if the police did not enforce it and did not tell the Germans, then they would get the beating and their lives would be in danger. So unfortunately, you know, they would have to-- have to divulge some of the names and make sure that, you know, person or two people are caught, you know, occasionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did-- that part, I really was left with a feeling of life as a-- you know, as a struggle. You know. I had to learn the other way. But as when you learn it as a young child, you cannot help it. But it gets embellished in your mind. You know. But I-- I never lost hope. And-- and again, I did whatever we-- I had to do to really survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1944-- in 1943, rather, rumors went around. There were-- the ghetto dwindled down to few people. By few I meant probably in the thousands, but a couple thousand. And we knew that it was going to be the end. I believe in 1943, it was declared a concentration camp, so it wasn't a ghetto anymore. It was officially a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about that? How was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my uncle was part of the committee, and he would come home and tell us all the latest news. And we had committee-- you know, the committee would have a-- a meeting for all the ghetto and tell us, you know, what-- what they wanted us to know, that this was going to happen, that was going to happen. But of course, to prevent panic breaking out, many things were not told to the mass of the people. But my uncle, being a committee member and knowing all they top people, the Jewish people, and the-- and the hierarchy, would alert us. So we, again, were forewarned in many instances as to what was going to happen. And so we were forewarned that the ghetto was going to be liquidated and that they were going to place all of us-- they were going to evacuate us all to Poland and Germany, to Stutthof, Dachau, you know, at the time we didn't know where. But eventually we knew it was to the camps. And-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So panic, really, I remember people becoming very, very panicky and-- and starting to to think of what to do, where to go, to plan, you know, how to-- how to survive. Was your family in a panic? What did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, my family was very much in a panic. And I remember we had the huddles-- the meetings that night and-- and starting to talk amongst, you know, everybody. And the children were involved, too, as to what to do. My-- my [INAUDIBLE], my uncle took his son out, who as at the time I think like three-- to a-- to a-- well, he must have been four, four and a half-- to a Lithuanian family. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go out in the workforce and hide-- you know, go-- take the child under a coat, under a jacket, or perhaps in a bag, you know, someplace that was-- that was hidden, and the workforce many times consisted of five, six people in a lineup. And so, again, by alerting the committee members, they would watch out that-- that they were in the middle and that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- when your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cousin was taken away. You remember this. By the--

speaker: interviewee
text: When my-- when my little boy was taken out of the ghetto? Yes, I do remember that. And-- well, and eventually he cried so hard with the-- with these Lithuanian people that they told him that he has to come and-- and pick him up because their lives were in danger. There were laws out that anyone who caught hiding a Jewish person or a child, the whole family would be annihilated, would be killed off. And they did make some examples of that. So, you know, they did actually catch people and -and killed off the whole family and had great publicity about that so that, you know, people wouldn't-- so that the Lithuanians or the Polish-descent people wouldn't be likely to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your little cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was brought back to the ghetto because he just was crying day and night, refused to eat, and, you know, they just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I remember that they-- my Aunt Paula was very clever woman, and they had many friends, Lithuanian friends, so they kept plotting what to do, how to do. And they asked me, you know, they said, look, your family, Ceitlin family, was so well-known in that district. Obviously you have people, you know, you know people. Don't you know any one of your parents' friends who might hide you? So I really didn't know. However, that piano that I mentioned to you that my parents placed, the black grand piano with these Polish people, and I would go down there and practice so I said, well, I think I remember how to go to their house. And I remember them, so maybe they'll take me. So this was in 1940-- December-- it was either December 1943 of January 1944. I-- they prepared me. I was quite tall, and I remember it was big excitement. I had-- they put makeup on me so that I would look older, because I was only 14. And I had high heels and-- and a coat. It was winter. I remember it was very cold. And I went out in the workforce and then-- we had-- we were-- Which you hadn't been doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was my first time that I was in the workforce. But, again, I approached everything as an exciting, you know, going to a-- some interesting adventure. We were wearing the [INAUDIBLE], you know, yellow [INAUDIBLE] back on-- on the left, so close to our heart, as they said, and on the-- in the back on the left side as well. And that was one of the-- one of the things that-- that we had to-- that my aunts coached me on how to take it off. And they were-- they were with-- they were with-- they were tacked on with-- with a pin so that when-- you know, I had to practice how to remove that back and-- from the front and from the back so that I would-- the only way you could go out of the workforce was to smuggle yourself out of the-- you know, to sort of position yourself in the front line and quickly find a place where you saw so many Lithuanians going by and throw yourself into that group of Lithuanians so that-- you know, without the Germans seeing you. Now there were-- I remember very distinctly, those Germans who were-- or the Lithuanians-- going every three rows. They were right there with a gun ready to shoot, which they did anytime someone escaped from the lineup. So I approached that, again, as an exciting event. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was never afraid. Somehow I-- it was just a feeling all along that I will survive. And I think that's more or less, I mean, when I think about it now and I have my grandchildren who are the same age, 11, 10, and they were children. But I was never a child, I knew exactly what I had to do, how I-- how I should do it, and I think that's what really-- one of the reasons for my survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you can imagine being without parents and having to-- and as much as my aunts were nice to me, they were really, you know, their first obligation was to their own children to-- for survival-- so I had to worry-- fend for myself. You know? So here you are in this workforce. How did you join the workforce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I immediately-- well, my uncle helped me. Again, without my uncle's help I would have never been able to-- to organize the whole thing. And so I went, you know, and I immediately took out my-- but I had to do all the thinking for myself, you know, because there was nobody. Once I went out in the workforce, nobody there to help me. And I had immediately taken off the [INAUDIBLE] or the star from my left side from the back, and then quickly took off the one from the left, from the front, and was just looking out, watching out where I could throw myself in with a Lithuanian group, which I did. I found a place and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you recognize where you were, what the neighborhood was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I recognized it, and it was close to-- to [INAUDIBLE] where my home was. And I went over there to-- to these people's home. I don't remember the name now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you run? How did you get away from your work group after you took the star off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I threw myself in with the Lithuanian people who were walking on the street, you know. They were out in the morning, going for shopping with bags and so on, and I had a handkerchief over me so I looked like a Lithuanian. When I had a handkerchief over my head. And-- and I just walked over to these people's home, and I found them. And, you know, they immediately cried and started to-- you know, took me in the room, and I told them. They had known-- they knew that my parents were sent out. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name-- unfortunately, I will have to provide you with her name, because I will tell you later around how I found them later on after the war. So I went over there, and I-- they cried and they hugged me and kissed me. And, you know, they told me that, you know, that-- that they knew about my parents and about my being all by myself. And they said, yes, they will. And I asked them if they would hide me because the ghetto is going to be liquidated, and they said, yes, they would.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thanked them, and I told them that I have to go back to the ghetto because I had forgotten some things to take out. I didn't-- I wasn't prepared to stay there. Well, I had to do the whole struggle to get back in the work lineup, which I did. And-- How did you manage it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, it was-- I had the two stars with me. I knew where the other people were working, and it wasn't very far. As I recall, it was like 10 minutes from their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were they doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were building a house or a factory. And they were picking up bricks and-- with mortar. I remember that. And so-- and some of the women were cooking, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] baking and-- and-- for the, you know, for the Lithuanians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back into the workforce and managed to get back into the ghetto. And I remember distinctly my Aunt Paula starting to shake me. What kind-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]? What kind of a child are you? Don't you realize the danger that--? You know, they-- they told-- they told you they will keep you. Stay there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no, the few things that I had left from my parents were very, very important to me. And so it was pictures and a-- a necklace that I had from my mother. And the necklace that I had from my mother, I don't think it was gold-plated. I still have it to this day. And so I said, well don't worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember my aunt really picking me up-- my Aunt Paula picking me up by my shoulders and starting to-- to actually jerk me and saying, what? Don't you understand? I said, don't worry. I'll go out tomorrow. I'll-- I'm fine. I'll just take the few things, and I'll leave. So I did that and, you know, said goodbye to them because before I didn't really say goodbye.

speaker: interviewee
text: And left the next morning, repeated the whole process and went in there, went to these people's home and they told me right away that people had-- that they had rumors. It was actually-- [INAUDIBLE] was a small suburb, and even though our house was very modern and-- to this day, and-- in the back, right the next street they were living primitive lives. I was really startled even when I went back now-- you know, six years later and now two years ago-- six years ago and two years ago, what primitive lives they are leading.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many of them still don't have any bathrooms. In that particular house where I was, there was-- there was no bathroom. And there was an outhouse outside. And what I wanted to tell you was that rumors went around right away. Did you see that Ceitlin girl? She was walking out in the street. And of course they risked their lives for me. So, you know, they said, no, we didn't see it. So I really had to go into hiding, be under very, very strict hiding conditions. And I-- And how did-- how did you do that? How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This family was also quite educated. They had two daughters who were seven-- who were seven years older than I was. One was seven. One was nine. So they were already in college. They were 20-- between 18 and 21. They were very nice to me. They gave me a-- I slept with the girls in the same room. They-- they appropriated a closet for me, and every time the doorbell rang, I would have to go in the closet and I would stay there in the closet, sometimes for hours and hours, until that person left. Of course they really restricted their visitation. You know, it was really a big sacrifice for these people. I-- they were of Polish descent. They-- they-- they had food, as I remember. They had a farm outside of-- of-- of [INAUDIBLE], this area where we lived, so they had all kinds of vegetables and meat and so on and so forth. So they treated me very nicely. The-- the mother was home, and she cooked every day and baked and various things. And-- however--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have any false paper because no one knew that I was there. I stayed there for six months. They-- as I said, I-- I was in the house. I could walk around in the house, but any time someone would knock on their door I would immediately go into that closet. They-- they had no bathroom inside, so I would sit there and with my legs crossed if I had to go for a bowel movement until the nighttime, when I could go out into the outhouse. And again, they would have to go out and survey all the area, that nobody was around at the time. And then I would go out and, you know, have my bowel movement and go back into the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: But again, I didn't-- I don't think that there were many days when I cried about my-- my-- my life, even though I had such a-- I was brought up with such a spoiled and with a golden spoon in my mouth. I-- I-- [INAUDIBLE] I took everything as though it's there and what am I going to do to survive, to better myself? Now, why do you think this family took you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that they were a really outstanding humanitarian people. At the time, I felt that perhaps it was as a payoff for the piano, which the children were also playing. They weren't very wealthy people. They were just plain folks, farm folks. But they were really, in thinking back, I-- I really cannot imagine how-- how wonderful, humanitarian people they were to risk their lives and their children's lives to hide a Jewish child, you know, was really incredible when I think about it. Well, I was there for about six months and in 19-- in June 1944 the Russians were back in Lithuania. And these people would tell me about the fires that they saw, ghetto, that the ghetto was being-- the people were liquidated, that they were-- they were sent out, they were taken by trains to Lithuania. I mean, to-- to Stutthof and Dachau-- and Dachau, Poland and Germany. My-- my whole family, was-- my aunts and uncles, they were all-- they all left the ghetto by trains, you know, and separated into Stutthof. Now my Aunt Chassia--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you tell us what happened to them, tell us a little more about what happened to you. You lived there for six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I lived there for six months. And they-- as I said, they were really very nice to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was frightened-- no, I was only frightened in incidences where-- where I heard shootings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about one.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I could see the Germans going around into-- into the streets with loudspeakers, announcing that anyone who is going to-- who is hiding a Jewish child-- you know, or Jewish people-- would be shot. And I can remember shootings. You know, I can remember gunshots. I would not look out the window, because, again, you know, I was-- they had curtains and I wasn't-- I was forbidden to-- to go near a window. They had-- these people had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] think it was a lady's [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] brother who was there. I was 14, and I remember that I-- since I didn't know anything about sex or about the reproductive system-- here and there, you know, from what we were-- what we heard from other children-- I remember that I got my period there. And I remember sitting on the couch and all of a sudden getting up and there was a big blood spot. And I got up, and I-- I felt so badly, and I took a towel, you know, tried to wash it off. Well, this-- this mother had a brother, and he was there. He wasn't working, I don't know why, but he was in the house all the time. And I remember that he was fondling me, but I couldn't say anything, you know, in fear that she would-- she didn't know. The woman didn't know. No one knew. And he told me don't say it to anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe how it happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since she was many times in the kitchen, the lady was in the kitchen, the man, her husband went off to work, the children were in school, so there was-- and he was home most of the time, and so, you know, we were sitting on the couch or he would go in the room or whatever and he would say, well let me feel you. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he touch you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He touched my breasts, touched my vagina, and, you know, I couldn't say anything. Here I was, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, I felt abused, but also I felt rather, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] excited, you know. I-- I really-- you know, I didn't know at the time that, you know, it was-- it was bad or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't afraid. But I-- I somehow didn't resist. You know, I'd-- I'd watch out that he was there, and I wouldn't go into that room, but, you know, eventually it was a small house. So that part..

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the mother aware of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was never aware of it, and the children, no one was ever aware of that because he told me don't say it to anybody. So-- [SIGHS] so I was there until June. In June, we heard that the Russians were coming back in.
